Member-only story

Api Design

Web Development

Software Engineering

Backend Development

Rest Api

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

You build a login flow, a payments page, and a real-time chat feature. You call all three “the API” and ship it to production.
But they aren’t the same thing.
Login is probably REST. If the chat actually works well, it’s probably not REST. Payments might be a 25-year-old SOAP service hiding under a sleek REST wrapper.
Most developers learn REST first. Because it’s the default, it becomes a hammer, and every engineering problem looks like a nail. Here’s the breakdown of what REST, GraphQL, Webhooks, WebSockets, gRPC, and SOAP actually do, and exactly when to use them.
What an API actually is
Your frontend shouldn’t care how your database is structured or what language your backend is written in. The API sits in the middle as a strict contract: send a request shaped like this, get a response shaped like that.
Every technology below is just a different philosophy on how that conversation should happen.
REST: the one everyone reaches for first
In REST, the URL tells you what you’re working with, and the HTTP method tells the server what to do with it.
Think of it like this:
GET /users/123 -> Fetch user 123
POST…
